1348	\subsection{Presheaf-level {\v C}ech machinery}
1349	\label{sec:presheaf_cech_machinery}
1350	
1351	The {\v C}ech-acyclicity of injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1352	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} below) is not a statement about a single
1353	affine cover: it is proved on the abelian category \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)
1354	of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, where the {\v C}ech complex of sections
1355	is the \(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex of an explicit free resolution. The argument
1356	rests on two presheaf-level facts, recorded here, and one transfer of injectivity.
1357	First, a free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of presheaves of modules whose
1358	\(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex into \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{is} the {\v C}ech complex
1359	of sections (Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex},
1360	Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} and
1361	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}). Second, the resolution property of
1362	that complex: \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) resolves \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\)
1363	concentrated in degree \(0\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso}). Together
1364	these give: an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is injective as a presheaf
1365	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} via
1366	Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint} applied to the sheafification adjunction
1367	Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}), so \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, \mathcal{I})\) is
1368	exact and carries the augmented resolution to an exact section complex, i.e.\
1369	\(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\). This direct route uses
1370	neither enough-injectives in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) nor a right-derived
1371	\(\delta\)-functor identification.
1372	
1373	\begin{definition}
1374	
1375	[Free presheaf complex of a cover]
1376	  \label{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1377	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreePresheafComplex,
1378	    AlgebraicGeometry.freeYoneda,
1379	    AlgebraicGeometry.coverOpen,
1380	    AlgebraicGeometry.coverInterOpen,

1385	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into}
1386	  % (the complex $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$) (read from
1387	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1142--1162, with the $j_{p!}$ description
1388	  % L1095--1135).
1389	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider the chain complex $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$
1390	  % of presheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
1391	  % $$
1392	  % \ldots
1393	  % \to
1394	  % \bigoplus_{i_0i_1i_2} (j_{i_0i_1i_2})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0i_1i_2}}
1395	  % \to
1396	  % \bigoplus_{i_0i_1} (j_{i_0i_1})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0i_1}}
1397	  % \to
1398	  % \bigoplus_{i_0} (j_{i_0})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0}}
1399	  % \to 0 \to \ldots
1400	  % $$
1401	  % where the last nonzero term is placed in degree $0$
1402	  % and where the map
1403	  % $$
1404	  % (j_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}}}
1405	  % \longrightarrow
1406	  % (j_{i_0\ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_{p + 1}})_{p!}
1407	  % \mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0\ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_{p + 1}}}
1408	  % $$
1409	  % is given by $(-1)^j$ times the canonical map."
1410	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology,
1411	  \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into} (the free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\)).}
1412	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an
1413	  open covering, with \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\)
1414	  the open immersion of each finite intersection. The Stacks formulation uses, for
1415	  each intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\), the \emph{extension-by-zero} presheaf
1416	  \((j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{!}\,\mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\), the presheaf of
1417	  modules sending an open \(W\) to \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) when
1418	  \(W \subset U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) and to \(0\) otherwise. This extension-by-zero
1419	  presheaf is, canonically, the \emph{free presheaf of modules on the representable}
1420	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\): writing
1421	  \(\mathbf{y}\,V = \operatorname{Hom}(-, V)\) for the Yoneda presheaf of the open
1422	  \(V\) on the site of opens of \(X\), the free presheaf of modules
1423	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V)\) sends \(W\) to the free
1424	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\)-module on the set \(\operatorname{Hom}(W, V)\), which is
1425	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) if \(W \subset V\) and \(0\) otherwise — exactly
1426	  extension-by-zero of \(\mathcal{O}_X|_V\). Its defining universal property
1427	  \[
1428	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}\bigl(
1429	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V),\, \mathcal{F}\bigr)
1430	    \;\cong\; \mathcal{F}(V)
1431	  \]
1432	  is the free–forgetful adjunction for presheaves of modules composed with the
1433	  Yoneda isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf{y}\,V, G) \cong G(V)\). The
1434	  \emph{free presheaf complex} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the chain complex
1435	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) in the abelian category
1436	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose
1437	  term in degree \(p\) is the direct sum
1438	  \[
1439	    K(\mathcal{U})_p
1440	      = \bigoplus_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
1441	        \operatorname{free}\bigl(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
1442	  \]
1443	  with \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) placed in degree \(p \geq 0\) (and \(0\) in negative
1444	  degrees), and whose differential \(K(\mathcal{U})_{p+1} \to K(\mathcal{U})_p\) is
1445	  the alternating sum \(\sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\) of the maps
1446	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}) \to
1447	  \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}})\)
1448	  obtained by applying \(\operatorname{free}\) to the representable index-dropping
1449	  maps \(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \to \mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
1450	  \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\) induced by the inclusions of opens
1451	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \subset U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\)
1452	  that drop the index \(i_j\). Each summand is a \emph{projective} object of
1453	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\), because the universal property above identifies
1454	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
1455	  i_p}), -)\) with the exact ``sections over \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)'' functor
1456	  \((-)(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\); this is the structural feature exploited below.
1457	
1458	  We assume the index set \(I\) is \emph{finite} (so the required coproducts exist in
1459	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\); this matches the finiteness hypothesis of the
1460	  comparison target). Here the direct sum \(\bigoplus\) is the categorical coproduct
1461	  \(\coprod\) (the indexed \(\operatorname{Sigma}\) colimit), which for a finite index
1462	  set coincides with the finite biproduct.
1463	\end{definition}

1467	[Section {\v C}ech complex of a presheaf of modules]
1468	  \label{def:section_cech_complex}
1469	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechComplex,
1470	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechCosimplicial}
1471	  \uses{def:cech_complex}
1472	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of presheaves,
1473	  % definition of $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
1474	  % (read from references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L879--910).
1475	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Set
1476	  % $$
1477	  % \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
1478	  % =
1479	  % \prod\nolimits_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p + 1}}
1480	  % \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0\ldots i_p}).
1481	  % $$
1482	  % This is an abelian group. ... We define
1483	  % $$
1484	  % d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
1485	  % \longrightarrow
1486	  % \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p + 1}(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
1487	  % $$
1488	  % by the formula
1489	  % $$
1490	  % d(s)_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}}
1491	  % =
1492	  % \sum\nolimits_{j = 0}^{p + 1}
1493	  % (-1)^j
1494	  % s_{i_0\ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_{p + 1}}|_{U_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}}}
1495	  % $$
1496	  % It is straightforward to see that $d \circ d = 0$."
1497	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of presheaves.}
1498	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an open
1499	  covering. For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{F}\), the
1500	  \emph{section {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
1501	  \mathcal{F})\) is the cochain complex of \emph{abelian groups} whose term in
1502	  degree \(p\) is the product of sections over the \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
1503	  \[
1504	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
1505	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
1506	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
1507	  \]
1508	  each factor being the value of the ``sections over \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)'' functor
1509	  (the evaluation of \(\mathcal{F}\) at the open \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)), with
1510	  differential the alternating sum of restriction maps
1511	  \[
1512	    d(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}
1513	      = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
1514	        s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}
1515	        \big|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}.
1516	  \]
1517	  This object is \emph{distinct} from the relative {\v C}ech complex
1518	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
1519	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex}: the latter is the pushforward complex with terms
1520	  \(\prod (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\) in
1521	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\), an object of relative sheaves over the base, whereas the
1522	  section {\v C}ech complex here is the simpler complex of \emph{global sections} of
1523	  \(\mathcal{F}\) over the intersections, an object of abelian groups (the underlying
1524	  additive structure of the sections). The two must not be conflated: the
1525	  presheaf-level acyclicity argument below runs entirely on this section complex.
1526	\end{definition}

1542	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, def:section_cech_complex}
1543	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into}
1544	  % (statement + proof) (read from references/stacks-cohomology.tex,
1545	  % L1163--1196).
1546	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Then there is an isomorphism
1547	  % $$
1548	  % \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet, \mathcal{F})
1549	  % =
1550	  % \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
1551	  % $$
1552	  % functorial in $\mathcal{F} \in \Ob(\textit{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X))$."
1553	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology,
1554	  \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into}.}
1555	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an
1556	  open covering. For every presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{F}\)
1557	  there is an isomorphism of cochain complexes of \emph{abelian groups}
1558	  \[
1559	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}\bigl(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet, \mathcal{F}\bigr)
1560	      \;=\;
1561	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}),
1562	  \]
1563	  natural in \(\mathcal{F} \in \mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\). Here the left side is
1564	  the cochain complex obtained by applying the contravariant
1565	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-, \mathcal{F})\) to the chain complex
1566	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex},
1567	  and the right side is the section {\v C}ech complex of
1568	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex}.
1569	\end{lemma}
1570	
1571	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We saw in the discussion just above the lemma that
1572	% $$
1573	% \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(
1574	% (j_{i_0\ldots i_p})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0\ldots i_p}},
1575	% \mathcal{F})
1576	% =
1577	% \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0\ldots i_p}).
1578	% $$
1579	% Hence we see that it is indeed the case that the direct sum
1580	% $$
1581	% \bigoplus\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_p}
1582	% (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}
1583	% $$
1584	% represents the functor
1585	% $$
1586	% \mathcal{F}
1587	% \longmapsto
1588	% \prod\nolimits_{i_0\ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0\ldots i_p}).
1589	% $$
1590	% Hence by Categories, Yoneda Lemma \ref{categories-lemma-yoneda}
1591	% we see that there is a complex $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$ with terms
1592	% as given. It is a simple matter to see that the maps are as given
1593	% in the lemma."
1594	\begin{proof}
1595	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, def:section_cech_complex}
1596	  The free–forgetful adjunction for presheaves of modules gives, composed with the
1597	  Yoneda isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf{y}\,V, \mathcal{F}) \cong
1598	  \mathcal{F}(V)\), the natural identification, for each multi-index,
1599	  \[
1600	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}\bigl(
1601	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}),\, \mathcal{F}
1602	    \bigr)
1603	    \;\cong\;
1604	    \operatorname{Hom}\bigl(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p},\,
1605	      \mathcal{F}\bigr)
1606	    \;\cong\;
1607	    \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}),
1608	  \]
1609	  where the last group is the evaluation of \(\mathcal{F}\) at the open
1610	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). Taking the product over multi-indices,
1611	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_p, \mathcal{F}) =
1612	  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}) =
1613	  \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\); the direct sum
1614	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) therefore represents the functor \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto
1615	  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\). Under this
1616	  identification the \((-1)^j\) maps \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0
1617	  \ldots i_{p+1}}) \to \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j}
1618	  \ldots i_{p+1}})\) defining the differential of \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) become
1619	  exactly the alternating-sum restriction differential of
1620	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\), so the two cochain
1621	  complexes coincide; naturality in \(\mathcal{F}\) is immediate from the
1622	  naturality of the free–forgetful adjunction and of Yoneda. The per-multi-index
1623	  bijection \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,
1624	  U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}), \mathcal{F}) \cong \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\) is additive,
1625	  so it is promoted to an isomorphism of abelian groups (an \emph{additive}
1626	  equivalence); taking the product over multi-indices and assembling these degreewise
1627	  additive isomorphisms — which commute with the alternating-sum differentials — yields
1628	  the complex isomorphism by matching components degree by degree.
1629	\end{proof}

1654	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}.}
1655	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an open
1656	  covering. The \emph{cover structure presheaf} \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}} :
1657	  X.\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}\) is the image presheaf of the augmentation
1658	  \[
1659	    \coprod_i \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_i) \;\longrightarrow\; \mathbf{1},
1660	  \]
1661	  where the target \(\mathbf{1} = \operatorname{PresheafOfModules.unit}(
1662	  X.\mathrm{ringCatSheaf}.\mathrm{obj})\) is the structure presheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\)
1663	  regarded as a presheaf of modules over itself. Concretely
1664	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(W) = \mathcal{O}_X(W)\) when \(W\) is contained in some
1665	  \(U_i\), and \(0\) otherwise. The degree-\(0\) term
1666	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_0 = \coprod_i \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_i)\) of the free
1667	  presheaf complex of Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} carries the
1668	  augmentation map \(K(\mathcal{U})_0 \to \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\) onto this image
1669	  presheaf.
1670	\end{definition}

1672	\begin{lemma}
1673	[Objectwise detection of quasi-isomorphisms of presheaf-of-module complexes]
1674	  \label{lem:quasiIso_of_evaluation}
1675	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.quasiIso_of_evaluation,
1676	    AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_Fmap_homologyMap,
1677	    AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_of_evaluation}
1678	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1679	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1680	  % (proof, the sectionwise-reduction step) (read from
1681	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1220--1226).
1682	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "It suffices to show that taking sections of this extended
1683	  % complex over any open $W \subset X$ leads to an acyclic complex."
1684	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1685	  (sectionwise reduction).}
1686	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of rings on a category \(C\) and let
1687	  \(\varphi : K \to L\) be a morphism of chain complexes in the abelian category
1688	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) of presheaves of \(R\)-modules. Suppose that for \emph{every}
1689	  object \(V\) the evaluated morphism — the image of \(\varphi\) under the functor
1690	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\bigr).
1691	  \operatorname{mapHomologicalComplex}\), a morphism of complexes of
1692	  \(R(V)\)-modules — is a quasi-isomorphism. Then \(\varphi\) is a
1693	  quasi-isomorphism. This is the objectwise-reduction step that turns the
1694	  resolution claim of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso} into a family of
1695	  sectionwise (combinatorial) statements: homology in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) is
1696	  computed open-by-open, because the evaluation functors
1697	  \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\) are exact (hence preserve
1698	  homology) and jointly conservative. The Lean entry point is
1699	  \(\operatorname{quasiIso\_of\_evaluation}\), which combines the joint
1700	  conservativity of the evaluations (each \(g\) is an isomorphism as soon as every
1701	  \((\operatorname{evaluation} R\, V).\operatorname{map} g\) is) with the fact that
1702	  each \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\) preserves homology.
1703	\end{lemma}
1704	
1705	\begin{proof}
1706	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1707	  A morphism of complexes is a quasi-isomorphism iff it induces an isomorphism on
1708	  homology in every degree (\(\operatorname{quasiIso\_iff}\),
1709	  \(\operatorname{quasiIsoAt\_iff\_isIso\_homologyMap}\)). Fix a degree \(i\). The
1710	  homology map \(H_i(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) iff each of
1711	  its evaluations is an isomorphism, since the underlying
1712	  presheaf-of-abelian-groups functor reflects isomorphisms and a morphism of
1713	  presheaves is an isomorphism iff it is so objectwise (joint conservativity). For
1714	  each \(V\), evaluation preserves homology, so the evaluation of \(H_i(\varphi)\)
1715	  is the homology map of the evaluated complex morphism; the latter is an
1716	  isomorphism because the evaluated morphism is a quasi-isomorphism by hypothesis.
1717	  Hence \(H_i(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism for all \(i\), i.e.\ \(\varphi\) is a
1718	  quasi-isomorphism.
1719	\end{proof}

1721	\begin{lemma}
1722	
1723	[Sectionwise description of the evaluated free complex]
1724	  \label{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise}
1725	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreeEval_X,
1726	    AlgebraicGeometry.freeYonedaEval_iso_of_le,
1727	    AlgebraicGeometry.freeYonedaEval_isZero_of_not_le}
1728	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, def:cover_structure_presheaf}
1729	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1730	  % (proof, the sectionwise description of $K^{ext}_\bullet(W)$) (read from
1731	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1227--1252).
1732	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Write $I = I_1 \amalg I_2$ where $W \subset U_i$ if and only
1733	  % if $i \in I_1$. ... If $I_1 \not = \emptyset$, then
1734	  % $\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U}(W) = \mathcal{O}_X(W)$ and
1735	  % $K^{ext}_p(W) = \bigoplus\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_p \in I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(W)$.
1736	  % This is true because of the simple description of the presheaves
1737	  % $(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}$."
1738	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1739	  (description of the evaluated complex).}
1740	  Fix an open \(V \subset X\) and write \(I_1 = I_1(V) = \{\,i \in I : V \leq U_i\,\}\)
1741	  for the set of cover members containing \(V\). Evaluating the free presheaf complex
1742	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} at
1743	  \(V\) gives, degreewise,
1744	  \[
1745	    K(\mathcal{U})_p(V)
1746	      = \bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I}
1747	        \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma)(V)
1748	      = \bigoplus_{\substack{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I \\ V \leq U_\sigma}}
1749	        \mathcal{O}_X(V)
1750	      = \bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(V),
1751	  \]
1752	  because \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,W)(V) = \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) when
1753	  \(V \leq W\) and \(0\) otherwise (poset hom-set is a subsingleton), and
1754	  \(V \leq U_\sigma = U_{\sigma(0)} \cap \cdots \cap U_{\sigma(p)}\) holds iff
1755	  \(\sigma\) takes all its values in \(I_1\). Under this identification the
1756	  differential of \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet(V)\) is the alternating-sum combinatorial
1757	  {\v C}ech differential of the full simplex on the index set \(I_1\) with
1758	  coefficients in the single module \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\). Moreover the evaluated
1759	  augmentation target is \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) when
1760	  \(I_1 \neq \varnothing\) and \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = 0\) when
1761	  \(I_1 = \varnothing\). Thus the evaluation at \(V\) of the augmented complex
1762	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet \to \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}[0]\)
1763	  (\(\operatorname{cechFreeComplexAug}\)) is precisely the augmented combinatorial
1764	  {\v C}ech complex of the simplex on \(I_1\) with coefficients \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\).
1765	  The Lean entry point for the degree-\(p\) unfolding is
1766	  \(\operatorname{cechFreePresheafComplex\_X}\) (which exposes
1767	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_p = \coprod_\sigma \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma)\) by
1768	  \(\operatorname{rfl}\)) composed with
1769	  \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation}\) at \(V\). The full degreewise
1770	  identification is assembled from three pieces: \(\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_X}\)
1771	  (evaluation at \(V\) commutes with the coproduct, giving
1772	  \((\operatorname{eval} V)(K(\mathcal{U})_p) \cong \coprod_\sigma
1773	  (\operatorname{eval} V)(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma))\)),
1774	  \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\) (a surviving summand with
1775	  \(V \leq U_\sigma\) is \(\cong \mathcal{O}_X(V)\)), and
1776	  \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_isZero\_of\_not\_le}\) (a summand with
1777	  \(V \not\leq U_\sigma\) is the zero object); collapsing the coproduct over the
1778	  surviving \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1\) gives
1779	  \(\bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(V)\).
1780	\end{lemma}
1781	
1782	\begin{proof}
1783	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, def:cover_structure_presheaf}
1784	  The degreewise identity is the value at \(V\) of the explicit description of the
1785	  free presheaf \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,W)\) recorded in
1786	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}: it sends \(V\) to the free
1787	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\)-module on \(\operatorname{Hom}(V, W)\), which is
1788	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) if \(V \leq W\) and \(0\) otherwise. Applying
1789	  \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation}\;V\) to the coproduct decomposition
1790	  \(\operatorname{cechFreePresheafComplex\_X}\) commutes with the direct sum and
1791	  collapses each summand to \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) or \(0\) according to whether
1792	  \(V \leq U_\sigma\), i.e.\ whether \(\sigma\) factors through \(I_1\); the surviving
1793	  summands are exactly those indexed by \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1\). The
1794	  evaluated faces are the index-dropping restriction maps, which on the surviving
1795	  summands are identities of \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\), so the alternating sum is the
1796	  combinatorial {\v C}ech differential. The augmentation value follows from
1797	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) exactly when some \(U_i\)
1798	  contains \(V\) (\(I_1 \neq \varnothing\)), recorded in
1799	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_structure_presheaf}.
1800	\end{proof}

1821	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1245--1268).
1822	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Moreover, the differential of the complex $K^{ext}_\bullet(W)$
1823	  % is given by
1824	  % $$
1825	  % d(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} =
1826	  % \sum\nolimits_{j = 0, \ldots, p + 1} \sum\nolimits_{i \in I_1}
1827	  % (-1)^j s_{i_0 \ldots i_{j - 1} i i_j \ldots i_p}.
1828	  % $$
1829	  % The sum is finite as the element $s$ has finite support.
1830	  % Fix an element $i_{\text{fix}} \in I_1$. Define a map
1831	  % $$
1832	  % h : K^{ext}_p(W) \longrightarrow K^{ext}_{p + 1}(W)
1833	  % $$
1834	  % by the rule
1835	  % $h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p + 1}} =
1836	  % \left\{ \begin{matrix}
1837	  % 0 & \text{if} & i_0 \not = i_{\text{fix}} \\
1838	  % s_{i_1 \ldots i_{p + 1}} & \text{if} & i_0 = i_{\text{fix}}
1839	  % \end{matrix} \right.$"
1840	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1841	  (differential and contracting homotopy of the evaluated complex).}
1842	  Fix a nonempty index set \(J\) (in the application \(J = I_1(V)\)), a chosen index
1843	  \(i_{\mathrm{fix}} \in J\), and a single coefficient module \(M\). The
1844	  \emph{constant-coefficient combinatorial {\v C}ech engine} consists of: the
1845	  alternating-sum {\v C}ech differential \(\operatorname{combDifferential}\) on the
1846	  full simplex over \(J\) with coefficients \(M\),
1847	  \[
1848	    (\operatorname{combDifferential} t)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}
1849	      = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\, t_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}},
1850	  \]
1851	  which squares to zero, \(\operatorname{combDifferential} \circ
1852	  \operatorname{combDifferential} = 0\) (\(\operatorname{combDifferential\_comp}\)); the
1853	  \emph{prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\)} homotopy \(\operatorname{combHomotopy}\),
1854	  \((\operatorname{combHomotopy}\, i_{\mathrm{fix}}\, u)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}
1855	  = u_{i_1 \ldots i_{p+1}}\) when \(i_0 = i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) and \(0\) otherwise; and
1856	  the contracting identity \(d \circ h + h \circ d = \mathrm{id}\)
1857	  (\(\operatorname{combHomotopy\_spec}\)), which yields positive-degree exactness
1858	  \(\operatorname{combDifferential\_exact}\) and the cocycle-splitting form
1859	  \(\operatorname{combDifferential\_eq\_of\_cocycle}\). The remaining declarations are
1860	  sign- and index-bookkeeping consumed by the contracting identity: the
1861	  succ-shift reindexing \(\operatorname{cons\_comp\_succAbove\_succ}\) and the
1862	  alternating-sign flip \(\operatorname{combSign\_flip}\), together with the
1863	  degenerate-case simplification \(\operatorname{combHomotopy\_zero}\). The generic
1864	  category-theory helper \(\operatorname{isZero\_sigma\_of\_forall\_isZero}\) (a
1865	  coproduct of zero objects is a zero object) is recorded here as well, used to collapse
1866	  the empty-index direct sums of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_empty}. This engine is
1867	  self-contained combinatorics on \(\operatorname{Fin.cons}\),

1869	  \(\operatorname{Finset.sum\_involution}\); it mirrors the contracting homotopy of the
1870	  Stacks \texttt{lemma-homology-complex} proof.
1871	\end{lemma}

2027	  Fix an open \(V\) with \(I_1(V) = \{\, i : V \leq U_i \,\} \neq \varnothing\). There
2028	  is an isomorphism of chain complexes of \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\)-modules
2029	  \[
2030	    \bigl((\operatorname{eval} V).\operatorname{mapHomologicalComplex}\bigr)
2031	      \bigl(\operatorname{cechFreePresheafComplex} \mathcal{U}\bigr)
2032	    \;\cong\;

2036	  \]

2038	  differential of \(C_\bullet\) is the constant-coefficient {\v C}ech differential
2039	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combDifferential}\) on the index set \(J = I_1(V)\)
2040	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:free_cech_engine}). The isomorphism is \emph{degreewise} and
2041	  \emph{intertwines the evaluated alternating-face differential with}
2042	  \(\operatorname{combDifferential}\). It is the chain/coproduct dual of the section-side
2043	  cochain identification of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}; isolating it
2044	  here gives the empty and nonempty cases (Lemmas~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_empty}
2045	  and~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_nonempty}) a common, ready degreewise model.

2048	\begin{proof}
2049	  \uses{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise, lem:free_cech_engine}
2050	  \emph{Degreewise.} By \(\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_X}\) of
2051	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise} evaluation at \(V\) commutes with the
2052	  coproduct,
2053	  \((\operatorname{eval} V)(K(\mathcal{U})_p) \cong \coprod_{\sigma :
2054	  \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \mathcal{U}.I_0} (\operatorname{eval} V)(\operatorname{free}
2055	  (\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma))\). Split the index set into the \(\sigma\) landing in
2056	  \(I_1(V)\) and the rest. Each summand with \(\sigma\) valued in \(I_1(V)\) (so
2057	  \(V \leq U_\sigma\)) is \(\cong \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) by
2058	  \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\); each remaining summand is the zero
2059	  object by \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_isZero\_of\_not\_le}\). Hence the coproduct
2060	  collapses to \(\coprod_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1(V)} \mathcal{O}_X(V) =
2061	  C_p\).
2062	
2063	  \emph{Differential match.} The evaluated differential is \((\operatorname{eval} V)\)
2064	  applied to the alternating face sum
2065	  \(\operatorname{AlternatingFaceMapComplex.objD}(\operatorname{cechFreeSimplicial}
2066	  \mathcal{U})\). The face \(\delta_i\) reindexes the \(\sigma\)-summand by
2067	  \(\sigma \mapsto \sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\) (the face action of
2068	  \(\operatorname{cechFreeSimplicial}\)); restricted to the
2069	  \(I_1\)-summands this is exactly the index-drop that the engine encodes, and the sign
2070	  \((-1)^i\) is supplied by \(\operatorname{objD}\). The match is verified on the
2071	  coproduct injections by \(\operatorname{Limits.Sigma.hom\_ext}\), reusing the
2072	  naturality of \(\operatorname{Limits.PreservesCoproduct.iso}\); summing with signs
2073	  identifies the evaluated differential with
2074	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combDifferential}\). This is the hard content of the
2075	  identification and the chain/coproduct dual of the cochain coface match in
2076	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}.
2077	
2078	  \emph{The three layers commuted past.} The degreewise iso is the \(3\)-fold
2079	  composite
2080	  \[
2081	    (\operatorname{eval} V)(K(\mathcal{U})_p)
2082	      \xrightarrow{\;\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_X}\;}
2083	      \coprod_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \mathcal{U}.I_0}
2084	        (\operatorname{eval} V)(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma))
2085	      \xrightarrow{\;\operatorname{cechFreeEvalDropZeros}\;}
2086	      \coprod_{V \leq U_\sigma} \mathcal{O}_X(V)
2087	      \xrightarrow{\;(\operatorname{Limits.Sigma.whiskerEquiv}\ \ldots)^{-1}\;}
2088	      C_p,
2089	  \]
2090	  whose layers are: (1) \(\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_X}\) = the comparison
2091	  \(\operatorname{Limits.PreservesCoproduct.iso}\) expressing that evaluation at
2092	  \(V\) commutes with the coproduct \(\coprod\); (2) \(\operatorname{cechFreeEvalDropZeros}\),
2093	  which kills the summands with \(V \not\leq U_\sigma\) (these evaluate to zero objects
2094	  by \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_isZero\_of\_not\_le}\)); (3)
2095	  \((\operatorname{Limits.Sigma.whiskerEquiv}\ \operatorname{survivingEquiv}\
2096	  (\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\, \ldots))^{-1}\), which reindexes the
2097	  surviving summands by \(\operatorname{survivingEquiv}\) of
2098	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_engine_complex} and identifies each evaluated summand with the
2099	  single coefficient \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) via \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\).
2100	
2101	  \emph{Naturality past each layer.} The reindex \(\operatorname{survivingEquiv}\) is
2102	  natural in the face map \(\sigma \mapsto \sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\):
2103	  a surviving index \(\sigma\) (i.e.\ \(V \leq U_\sigma\), equivalently
2104	  \(V \leq \operatorname{coverInterOpen}\, \mathcal{U}\, \sigma\) by
2105	  \(\operatorname{le\_coverInterOpen\_iff}\)) is carried to a surviving
2106	  \(\sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\) (still \(V \leq U_{\sigma \circ
2107	  \operatorname{succAbove}\, i}\), since dropping a coordinate only weakens the meet),
2108	  so the drop-zeros layer (2) and the whisker layer (3) commute with every face. On a
2109	  surviving summand the evaluated transition \(\operatorname{freeYoneda}.\mathrm{map}
2110	  (\operatorname{homOfLE}\, \ldots)\) collapses to the identity under
2111	  \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\); hence each face \(\delta_i\) matches the
2112	  engine reindexing \(\sigma \mapsto \sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\)
2113	  summand-for-summand, and summing with the \(\operatorname{objD}\) signs \((-1)^i\)
2114	  identifies the evaluated alternating-face differential with
2115	  \(\operatorname{cechEngineD}\) (\(= \) the chain transpose of
2116	  \(\operatorname{combDifferential}\)).
2117	
2118	  \emph{The genuine difficulty: variance.} The free \emph{chain} differential
2119	  \emph{lowers} \(\mathrm{Fin}\)-arity (\(\sigma \mapsto \sigma \circ
2120	  \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\), faces), whereas
2121	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combDifferential}\) \emph{raises} it (it inserts an
2122	  index, summing over \(i \in I_1\)). The two are reconciled because on the finite
2123	  biproduct \(\coprod = \prod\) the insertion/deletion adjunction makes the index-drop
2124	  chain differential the transpose of the index-raise cochain differential; this
2125	  transpose is precisely what \(\operatorname{cechEngineD}\) of
2126	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_engine_complex} packages, and verifying the commutation amounts to
2127	  the injectionwise check \(\operatorname{Limits.Sigma.hom\_ext}\) outlined above.
2128	\end{proof}

2261	  (the contracting identity).}

2264	  prepend homotopy \(h\) satisfies the contracting identity
2265	  \[
2266	    d \circ h + h \circ d = \mathrm{id}
2267	  \]
2268	  on the augmented evaluated complex
2269	  \(\cdots \to K(\mathcal{U})_1(V) \to K(\mathcal{U})_0(V) \to
2270	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) \to 0\) (including the degree-\(0\)/degree-\((-1)\)

2276	  \uses{lem:cech_free_eval_prepend_homotopy, lem:free_cech_engine}
2277	  This is the same alternating-sum cancellation as
2278	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combHomotopy\_spec}\)
2279	  (\(\operatorname{combDifferential}(\operatorname{combHomotopy} r\, t) +
2280	  \operatorname{combHomotopy} r\, (\operatorname{combDifferential} t) = t\)),

2282	  \((dh + hd)(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\): the \(j = 0\) term of \(dh\) prepends and then
2283	  removes \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\), returning \(s_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\); every remaining term
2284	  of \(dh\) cancels in pairs against the corresponding term of \(hd\) (the
2285	  prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) of the differential), because removing an index
2286	  \(j \geq 1\) after prepending \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) equals prepending
2287	  \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) after removing index \(j-1\), with opposite signs. The net
2288	  result is \(s_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\), i.e.\ \(dh + hd = \mathrm{id}\); the index
2289	  bookkeeping is the \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.cons\_comp\_succAbove\_succ}\) /
2290	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combSign\_flip}\) content of
2291	  Lemma~\ref{lem:free_cech_engine}.

2447	\begin{lemma}[Right adjoint of a mono-preserving left adjoint preserves injectives]
2448	  \label{lem:injective_of_adjoint}

2453	  Let \(L \dashv R\) be an adjunction between abelian (or, more generally, suitable)
2454	  categories such that the left adjoint \(L\) preserves monomorphisms. Then for every
2455	  injective object \(J\) of the target, \(R(J)\) is an injective object of the source.

2461	  \mathlibok

2464	  For the identity ring map \(\alpha = \mathbf{1}\) over a scheme \(X\),

2544	\begin{lemma}
2545	[Hom into an injective preserves exactness and quasi-isomorphisms]
2546	  \label{lem:hom_into_injective_exact}

2550	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category and \(I\) an injective object. Then the
2551	  contravariant hom functor \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, I) =
2552	  \operatorname{preadditiveYoneda}.\mathrm{obj}\,I : \mathcal{A}^{\mathrm{op}} \to
2553	  \mathrm{Ab}\) is exact: it is automatically left exact (preserves finite limits), and
2554	  injectivity of \(I\) makes it carry epimorphisms to epimorphisms, so it preserves
2555	  finite colimits (\(\operatorname{preadditiveYoneda\_obj\_preservesFiniteColimits\_of\_injective}\))
2556	  and hence preserves homology. Consequently \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, I)\) carries
2557	  quasi-isomorphisms of chain complexes in \(\mathcal{A}^{\mathrm{op}}\) to
2558	  quasi-isomorphisms of the \(\operatorname{Hom}\)-cochain complexes
2559	  (\(\operatorname{quasiIso\_map\_preadditiveYoneda\_of\_injective}\)). Both statements
2560	  are for a general abelian category, the right generality for reuse in
2561	  Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic}. The mechanism --- a right adjoint / exact
2562	  contravariant functor preserves the relevant exactness --- is the same homological
2563	  principle behind Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint}.
2564	\end{lemma}

2570	  gives that \(\operatorname{preadditiveYoneda}.\mathrm{obj}\,I\) preserves
2571	  epimorphisms. Combined with the automatic preservation of finite limits, the
2572	  short-exact-sequence criterion
2573	  \(\operatorname{Functor.preservesFiniteColimits\_iff\_forall\_exact\_map\_and\_epi}\)
2574	  shows it preserves finite colimits, hence (being also left exact) it has
2575	  \(\operatorname{PreservesHomology}\) by
2576	  \(\operatorname{Functor.preservesHomologyOfExact}\). A homology-preserving functor
2577	  sends quasi-isomorphisms to quasi-isomorphisms degreewise
2578	  (\(\operatorname{quasiIsoAt\_map\_of\_preservesHomology}\)), which is the second
2579	  statement.

2649	
2650	  \emph{Part 1: an injective sheaf is injective as a presheaf.} Sheafification is
2651	  left adjoint to the inclusion
2652	  \(\iota : \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \hookrightarrow \mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)
2653	  of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules into presheaves of
2654	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}), and its left
2655	  adjoint sheafification is exact, hence preserves monomorphisms. By
2656	  Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint} the right adjoint \(\iota\) carries injective
2657	  objects to injective objects; thus an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
2658	  \(\mathcal{I}\) is injective as an object of \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\). In
2659	  particular the contravariant functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-,
2660	  \mathcal{I})\) is exact on \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
2661	
2662	  \emph{Part 2: vanishing.} By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso} the free
2663	  presheaf complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of
2664	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} is a resolution of
2665	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\) concentrated in degree \(0\); equivalently the
2666	  augmented complex \(\cdots \to K(\mathcal{U})_1 \to K(\mathcal{U})_0 \to
2667	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}} \to 0\) is exact in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
2668	  Applying the exact functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-, \mathcal{I})\)
2669	  of Part 1 to this exact augmented complex yields an exact complex; by the
2670	  identification \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet,
2671	  \mathcal{I}) = \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I})\) of
2672	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification} (with the section {\v C}ech complex
2673	  of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex}), this is precisely the statement that
2674	  the section {\v C}ech complex of \(\mathcal{I}\) is exact in positive degrees, with
2675	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}, \mathcal{I}) =
2676	  \mathcal{I}(U)\) supplying degree \(0\).
2677	
2678	  \emph{Categorical assembly.} Concretely the exactness is produced by the
2679	  opposite-transport bridge. The augmentation \(\operatorname{cechFreeComplexAug}
2680	  \mathcal{U}\) is a quasi-isomorphism (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso});
2681	  taking its opposite via the \(\operatorname{HomologicalComplex.op}\) machinery and
2682	  applying \(\operatorname{preadditiveYoneda}.\mathrm{obj}\,\mathcal{I}\) keeps it a
2683	  quasi-isomorphism, since \(\mathcal{I}\) is injective as a presheaf of modules
2684	  (Part 1) and \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, \mathcal{I})\) then preserves homology
2685	  (\(\operatorname{quasiIso\_map\_preadditiveYoneda\_of\_injective}\),
2686	  Lemma~\ref{lem:hom_into_injective_exact}). Transporting source and target of this
2687	  mapped-opposite quasi-isomorphism across the opposite-transport isos
2688	  \(\operatorname{homCechComplexMapOpIso}\)
2689	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_op_identification}) and
2690	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechComplexMapOpIso}\)
2691	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_complex_mapop_iso}) identifies it with the augmentation
2692	  of the section {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
2693	  \mathcal{I})\) onto the mapped image of the degree-\(0\)-concentrated target
2694	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}[0]\). The latter has vanishing homology in positive
2695	  degrees, so the section {\v C}ech complex does too. Hence
2696	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\) and
2697	  \(\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = \mathcal{I}(U)\). This direct route uses
2698	  neither enough-injectives in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) nor a right-derived
2699	  \(\delta\)-functor identification.
2700	\end{proof}

2770	  \mathcal{F}) = 0\) and (b) each restriction \(s|_{U_i}\) lifts to a section
2771	  \(s_i \in \mathcal{G}(U_i)\); both can be arranged simultaneously because the

2790	\section{Absolute sheaf cohomology as Ext of the corepresenting object}
2791	
2792	The two vanishing lemmas below speak about the \emph{absolute} sheaf cohomology
2793	\(H^p(U, \mathcal{F})\) of an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module over an open \(U\). Their
2794	dimension-shift proof needs three structural facts about \(H^p(U, -)\): a degree-zero
2795	identification with global sections, vanishing on injective modules, and a long exact
2796	sequence attached to a short exact sequence of modules. We realize \(H^p(U, -)\) as the
2797	\emph{Ext of a corepresenting object} in the single abelian category
2798	\(X.\mathrm{Modules} = \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\): there is an
2799	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) corepresenting the
2800	sections-over-\(U\) functor \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\), and we set
2801	\(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = \operatorname{Ext}^p_{\mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}(j_!\mathcal{O}_U,
2802	\mathcal{F})\). The corepresenting object is built \emph{without} the extension-by-zero
2803	functor — it is the sheafification of the free module on the representable presheaf
2804	\(\mathbf{y}\,U\) — and the realization keeps the injective module in the \emph{second}
2805	Ext argument, where Mathlib supplies all three facts off the shelf with \emph{no} appeal
2806	to restriction of module sheaves. The corepresenting object and its corepresentability
2807	are fixed first, then the following definition fixes the realization, and the Mathlib
2808	dependency anchors after it pin the exact results it rests on.
2809	
2810	\begin{definition}
2811	
2812	\leanok
2813	[Extension-by-zero structure sheaf as a corepresenting object]
2814	  \label{def:jshriek_ou}
2815	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.jShriekOU}
2816	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction,
2817	    lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}
2818	  \textit{Corepresenting object for sections over an open, built without the
2819	  extension-by-zero functor.}
2820	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open subscheme, regarded through the
2821	  representable presheaf \(\mathbf{y}\,U\) on the site of opens of \(X\). The
2822	  \emph{extension-by-zero structure sheaf} \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) is the object of
2823	  \(X.\mathrm{Modules} = \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) obtained by sheafifying the free
2824	  presheaf of modules on \(\mathbf{y}\,U\):
2825	  \[
2826	    j_!\mathcal{O}_U \;:=\; \operatorname{sheafification}\bigl(
2827	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U)\bigr)
2828	    \;\in\; X.\mathrm{Modules},
2829	  \]
2830	  where \(\operatorname{free}\) is the free presheaf-of-modules functor on the
2831	  representable (the degree-zero generator of the free presheaf complex of
2832	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}) and
2833	  \(\operatorname{sheafification} \dashv \iota\) is the sheafification adjunction of
2834	  Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}. Up to isomorphism this is the classical
2835	  extension-by-zero \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) of the structure sheaf of \(U\) along the open
2836	  immersion \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\); crucially, we build only this \emph{object} as a
2837	  sheafified free module, never the extension-by-zero \emph{functor} \(j_!\). The object
2838	  exists unconditionally: the free presheaf of modules on a representable and the
2839	  sheafification of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules are both supplied by Mathlib.
2840	\end{definition}
2841	
2842	\begin{lemma}
2843	
2844	[Corepresentability of global sections over \(U\)]
2845	  \label{lem:jshriek_corepr}
2846	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.jShriekOU_homEquiv, AlgebraicGeometry.sheafificationHomAddEquiv}
2847	  \uses{def:jshriek_ou, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction, lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}
2848	  \textit{The object \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) corepresents the sections-over-\(U\) functor.}
2849	  For every \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) there is a natural additive
2850	  isomorphism
2851	  \[
2852	    \operatorname{Hom}_{X.\mathrm{Modules}}\bigl(j_!\mathcal{O}_U,\, \mathcal{F}\bigr)
2853	    \;\cong\;
2854	    \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F}) \;=\; \mathcal{F}(U),
2855	  \]
2856	  natural in \(\mathcal{F}\). Thus \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) corepresents the global-sections
2857	  functor \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\) on \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\).
2858	\end{lemma}
2859	
2860	\begin{proof}
2861	  \uses{def:jshriek_ou, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction, lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}
2862	  Compose two natural additive isomorphisms. The sheafification adjunction
2863	  \(\operatorname{sheafification} \dashv \iota\) of
2864	  Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction} transports corepresentability through
2865	  sheafification:
2866	  \[
2867	    \operatorname{Hom}_{X.\mathrm{Modules}}\bigl(
2868	      \operatorname{sheafification}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U)),\, \mathcal{F}
2869	    \bigr)
2870	    \;\cong\;
2871	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}\bigl(
2872	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U),\, \iota\mathcal{F}\bigr).
2873	  \]
2874	  The free–Yoneda additive equivalence \(\operatorname{freeYonedaHomAddEquiv}\) of
2875	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification} then identifies the right-hand
2876	  presheaf Hom-group with the evaluation \((\iota\mathcal{F})(U) = \mathcal{F}(U) =
2877	  \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\):
2878	  \[
2879	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}\bigl(
2880	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U),\, \iota\mathcal{F}\bigr)
2881	    \;\cong\;
2882	    \mathcal{F}(U).
2883	  \]
2884	  Both isomorphisms are additive and natural in \(\mathcal{F}\), so their composite is
2885	  the asserted natural additive isomorphism.
2886	\end{proof}

2888	\begin{definition}
2889	
2890	\leanok
2891	[Absolute sheaf cohomology as Ext of the corepresenting object]
2892	  \label{def:absolute_cohomology}
2893	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.absoluteCohomology}
2894	  \uses{def:jshriek_ou, lem:jshriek_corepr, lem:ext_bifunctor_mathlib,
2895	    lem:ext_covariant_les_mathlib, lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib,
2896	    lem:ext_homequiv_zero_mathlib, lem:hasext_standard_mathlib}
2897	  \textit{Realization of absolute sheaf cohomology in the module-sheaf category.}
2898	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(U \subseteq
2899	  X\) an open subscheme with open immersion \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\). Let
2900	  \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U \in X.\mathrm{Modules}\) be the corepresenting object of
2901	  Definition~\ref{def:jshriek_ou}. The \emph{absolute sheaf cohomology of \(\mathcal{F}\)
2902	  over \(U\)} is defined to be the Ext of this corepresenting object in the single
2903	  abelian category \(X.\mathrm{Modules} = \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of sheaves of
2904	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules:
2905	  \[
2906	    H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) \;:=\; \operatorname{Ext}^p_{\mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}
2907	      \bigl(j_!\mathcal{O}_U,\; \mathcal{F}\bigr),
2908	    \qquad p \geq 0.
2909	  \]
2910	  Both Ext arguments live in \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\); no restriction of \(\mathcal{F}\)
2911	  along \(j\) is taken. Here \(\operatorname{Ext}\) is Mathlib's Ext bifunctor
2912	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_bifunctor_mathlib}) on \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\); the
2913	  \(\mathrm{HasExt}\) structure it requires is available unconditionally by
2914	  Lemma~\ref{lem:hasext_standard_mathlib}. The three structural facts the downstream
2915	  dimension-shift arguments consume are then exactly the corresponding Mathlib results:
2916	  \begin{enumerate}
2917	  \item \textbf{Degree zero is global sections.} In degree \(0\), Ext is the Hom-group,
2918	    \(\operatorname{Ext}^0(j_!\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{F}) \cong
2919	    \operatorname{Hom}_{X.\mathrm{Modules}}(j_!\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{F})\), by the
2920	    degree-zero Ext comparison (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_homequiv_zero_mathlib}); composing
2921	    with the corepresentability isomorphism
2922	    \(\operatorname{Hom}_{X.\mathrm{Modules}}(j_!\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{F}) \cong
2923	    \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:jshriek_corepr} gives \(H^0(U,
2924	    \mathcal{F}) \cong \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\).
2925	  \item \textbf{Injective vanishing.} When the \emph{second} Ext argument is an
2926	    injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{I}\), one has
2927	    \(\operatorname{Ext}^{n+1}(-, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) by
2928	    Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib}. Since \(\mathcal{I}\) is exactly
2929	    the second argument of \(\operatorname{Ext}^{n+1}(j_!\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{I})\),
2930	    this yields \(H^{n+1}(U, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) directly: the short exact sequence stays
2931	    in \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\), no restriction is taken, and
2932	    restriction-preserves-injectives is not needed.
2933	  \item \textbf{Long exact sequence.} For a short exact sequence \(0 \to \mathcal{F}
2934	    \to \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0\) in \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\) and the
2935	    \emph{fixed} first argument \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\), the covariant Ext long exact
2936	    sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_covariant_les_mathlib}) is the long exact sequence of
2937	    the groups \(H^p(U, -)\), with connecting maps given by composition with the Ext
2938	    class of the extension.
2939	  \end{enumerate}
2940	\end{definition}

2967	\begin{lemma}
2968	[Degree-zero Ext is the Hom-group]
2969	  \label{lem:ext_homequiv_zero_mathlib}
2970	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.homEquiv₀,

3055	  \label{lem:absolute_cohomology_zero_natural}

3062	  \textit{Project-bespoke obligation.}
3063	  The isomorphism \(H^0(U, -) \cong \Gamma(U, -)\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_zero} is
3064	  natural in the coefficient sheaf: for a morphism \(g : \mathcal{F}_1 \to \mathcal{F}_2\) in
3065	  \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\) the square
3066	  \[
3067	    \begin{array}{ccc}
3068	      H^0(U, \mathcal{F}_1) & \longrightarrow & H^0(U, \mathcal{F}_2)\\
3069	      \cong\downarrow & & \downarrow\cong\\
3070	      \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F}_1) & \xrightarrow{\;g_U\;} & \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F}_2)
3071	    \end{array}
3072	  \]
3073	  commutes, where the top arrow is \(H^0(U, g)\), the functorial action of \(g\) on
3074	  \(\operatorname{Ext}^0\) in the second variable, and the bottom arrow is the sections map
3075	  \(g_U = g(U)\).

3080	  corresponds to post-composition by \(g\) in \(\operatorname{Hom}_{X.\mathrm{Modules}}(j_!
3081	  \mathcal{O}_U, -)\); under the corepresentability isomorphism of Lemma~\ref{lem:jshriek_corepr}
3082	  (which is natural in the second variable, being a composite of the sheafification adjunction and
3083	  the free–Yoneda evaluation, both natural), this in turn corresponds to applying \(g\) on sections
3084	  over \(U\), i.e. \(g_U\). Hence the square commutes. In particular, when \(g_U\) is surjective so
3085	  is \(H^0(U, g)\) — the transfer used by the surjectivity step of
3086	  Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_one_vanishing}.

3184	  system \(s\) of Definition~\ref{def:affine_cover_system}: its basis \(\mathcal{B}\) is the
3185	  standard (distinguished) opens of the affine \(U\) and its admissible coverings \(\mathrm{Cov}\) are
3186	  the standard open covers, the three nontrivial fields being discharged by

3201	\subsection{The affine instantiation (Tag 02KG)}
3202	
3203	The Serre vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} is obtained by feeding a concrete
3204	\emph{affine} cover system into the basis-comparison criterion. We construct that cover system
3205	(Definition~\ref{def:affine_cover_system}) and discharge its three nontrivial fields, then supply the
3206	quasi-coherent seed that meets condition (3). The sub-lemmas below are the formalize-ready cut points
3207	of this instantiation.
3208	
3209	\begin{lemma}
3210	[Distinguished opens are closed under finite intersection]
3211	  \label{lem:affine_faces_mem}
3212	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.affine_faces_mem}
3213	  \uses{def:standard_affine_cover}
3214	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Lemma \texttt{lemma-standard-open}
3215	  % (surrounding prose on standard opens) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex,
3216	  % L513--517).
3217	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that $\Spec(R)$ has a basis of open sets $D(f)$,
3218	  % $f \in R$ which we call standard opens, see Algebra,
3219	  % Definition \ref{algebra-definition-Zariski-topology}.
3220	  % In addition, the intersection of two standard opens is another:
3221	  % $D(f) \cap D(g) = D(fg)$, $f, g\in R$."
3222	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-standard-open}
3223	  (standard opens).}
3224	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) the associated affine scheme. The
3225	  distinguished opens \(D(f)\), \(f \in R\), form a basis closed under intersection: \(D(f) \cap D(g)
3226	  = D(fg)\). Consequently, for any standard open cover \(\mathcal{U} : V = \bigcup_{i \in \iota}
3227	  D(g_i)\) of a distinguished open \(V\), every finite intersection \(D(g_{i_0}) \cap \cdots \cap
3228	  D(g_{i_p}) = D(g_{i_0} \cdots g_{i_p})\) of its members is again a distinguished open. This is the
3229	  \(\mathrm{faces\_mem}\) field (condition (1)) of the affine cover system.
3230	\end{lemma}
3231	\begin{proof}
3232	  \uses{def:standard_affine_cover}
3233	  The identity \(D(f) \cap D(g) = D(fg)\) holds because a prime \(\mathfrak{p}\) avoids both \(f\) and
3234	  \(g\) iff it avoids the product \(fg\). Iterating over the indices \(i_0, \ldots, i_p\) of a face
3235	  gives \(\bigcap_k D(g_{i_k}) = D\bigl(\prod_k g_{i_k}\bigr)\), a distinguished open; the base set
3236	  \(V = D(f)\) is distinguished by hypothesis. Hence every face of a standard cover lies in the basis.
3237	\end{proof}
3238

3243	  \uses{def:standard_affine_cover}

3245	  % and following prose (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L531--532,
3246	  % L573--577).
3247	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Any open covering of $D(f)$ can be refined to a finite
3248	  % open covering of the form $D(f) = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(g_i)$."
3249	  % SOURCE QUOTE (cofinality suffices): "Moreover,
3250	  % we showed in
3251	  % Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-cofinal-systems-coverings-standard-case}
3252	  % that it is sufficient to check the sheaf
3253	  % condition on a cofinal system of open coverings for each
3254	  % standard open. By the lemma above it suffices to check
3255	  % on the finite coverings by standard opens."
3256	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Sheaves on Spaces, Tag 009L, Lemma
3257	  % \texttt{lemma-cofinal-systems-coverings-standard-case} (read from

3259	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a topological space. Let $\mathcal{B}$ be a basis
3260	  % for the topology on $X$. Assume that for every triple
3261	  % $U, U', U'' \in \mathcal{B}$ with $U' \subset U$ and $U'' \subset U$ we have
3262	  % $U' \cap U'' \in \mathcal{B}$. For each $U \in \mathcal{B}$, let

3267	  % \item The presheaf $\mathcal{F}$ is a sheaf on $\mathcal{B}$.

3275	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-standard-open}~(2), and
3276	  Sheaves on Spaces, Tag 009L, \texttt{lemma-cofinal-systems-coverings-standard-case}.}
3277	  Let \(V = D(f)\) be a distinguished open of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\). The standard open covers of
3278	  \(V\) — the finite coverings \(V = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(g_i)\) by distinguished opens — form a cofinal
3279	  system among all open coverings of \(V\): every open covering of \(V\) admits a refinement that is a
3280	  standard open cover. Together with the closure of the basis under intersection
3281	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_faces_mem}), this is the cofinality input that the sheaf-on-a-basis principle
3282	  (Tag 009L) requires.

3312	  Let \(V\) be a distinguished open of the affine and let \(S : 0 \to S_1 \to S_2 \to S_3 \to 0\) be a
3313	  short exact sequence of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose left term \(S_1\) has vanishing positive
3314	  {\v C}ech cohomology over the standard covers of \(V\), i.e.\ \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, S_1) = 0\)

3328	\begin{lemma}
3329	[Covering-datum bridge to the open-cover {\v C}ech form]
3330	  \label{lem:cover_datum_bridge}
3331	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverDatum_bridge}
3332	  \uses{def:basis_cov_system}
3333	  \textit{Project-bespoke compatibility lemma.}
3334	  The injective {\v C}ech-acyclicity Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} is stated over an
3335	  open-cover object with a finiteness hypothesis on the index set, whereas the affine cover system's
3336	  admissible coverings are raw covering data \((\iota, U)\) (\(\mathrm{CovDatum}\), with \(\iota\)
3337	  finite, of Definition~\ref{def:basis_cov_system}). This lemma exhibits, for each finite standard
3338	  covering datum, the canonical identification of its section {\v C}ech complex with the section
3339	  {\v C}ech complex of the corresponding open-cover object, so that the {\v C}ech cohomology computed
3340	  in the two presentations agrees.
3341	\end{lemma}
3342	\begin{proof}
3343	  \uses{def:basis_cov_system}
3344	  A standard covering datum \((\iota, U)\) with \(\iota\) finite is precisely the data of a finite
3345	  family of opens \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) whose union is the covered open; this is the
3346	  same underlying data as the open-cover object carried by
3347	  Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic}. The degreewise products
3348	  \(\prod_\sigma \mathcal{F}(V_\sigma)\) defining the two section {\v C}ech complexes range over the
3349	  same index tuples and use the same face opens \(V_\sigma\), and the coface maps coincide; hence the
3350	  two complexes are identified and their cohomologies agree.
3351	\end{proof}

3414	\begin{lemma}
3415	[Quasi-coherent sheaves on an affine are sections-tilde]
3416	  \label{lem:qcoh_iso_tilde_sections}
3417	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.qcoh_iso_tilde_sections}
3418	  \uses{def:standard_affine_cover}
3419	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Tag 01HV, Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves}
3420	  % (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L691--719).
3421	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$
3422	  % be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
3423	  % associated to $M$.
3424	  % \begin{enumerate}
3425	  % \item We have $\Gamma(\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}) = R$.
3426	  % \item We have $\Gamma(\Spec(R), \widetilde M) = M$ as an $R$-module.
3427	  % \item For every $f \in R$ we have
3428	  % $\Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}) = R_f$.
3429	  % \item For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
3430	  % as an $R_f$-module.
3431	  % \end{enumerate}"
3432	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, Tag 01HV, \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves}.}
3433	  Let \(X = \operatorname{Spec} R\) be an affine scheme and \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
3434	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Writing \(M = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\) for its module of global sections,
3435	  there is a natural isomorphism \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{M}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules,
3436	  under which \(\Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F}) = M_f\) for every \(f \in R\); in particular the sections of
3437	  \(\mathcal{F}\) over a distinguished open \(D(f)\) are the away localisation \(M_f\). This is the
3438	  affine quasi-coherent structure theorem (Tag 01HV, with the equivalence quasi-coherent-on-affine
3439	  \(\simeq\) \(R\)-modules, Tag 01I8).
3440	\end{lemma}
3441	\begin{proof}
3442	  \uses{def:standard_affine_cover}
3443	  On an affine scheme the global-sections functor \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\) and
3444	  the tilde functor \(M \mapsto \widetilde{M}\) are mutually inverse equivalences between
3445	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and \(R\)-modules; the counit
3446	  \(\widetilde{\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})} \to \mathcal{F}\) is an isomorphism for quasi-coherent
3447	  \(\mathcal{F}\). By Tag 01HV the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over \(D(f)\) are \(M_f\), so the
3448	  isomorphism identifies \(\Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F})\) with \(M_f\).
3449	\end{proof}

3451	\begin{lemma}
3452	[Standard-cover {\v C}ech vanishing for quasi-coherent coefficients]
3453	  \label{lem:affine_cech_vanishing_qcoh}
3454	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.affine_cech_vanishing_qcoh}
3455	  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine, lem:qcoh_iso_tilde_sections, def:affine_cover_system,
3456	    def:has_vanishing_higher_cech}
3457	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KG, Lemma
3458	  % \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} (proof, condition (3)) (read
3459	  % from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L172--173).
3460	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Finally, condition (3) of the lemma follows from
3461	  % Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}."
3462	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KG
3463	  (\texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}, proof).}
3464	  Let \(X = \operatorname{Spec} R\) be an affine scheme and \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
3465	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then \(\mathcal{F}\) has vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for the
3466	  affine cover system (Definition~\ref{def:affine_cover_system},
3467	  Definition~\ref{def:has_vanishing_higher_cech}): for every standard cover \(\mathcal{U}\) and every
3468	  \(p > 0\) one has \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\). This is the seed that supplies
3469	  condition (3) of the basis-comparison Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}, and is the 01I8
3470	  globalisation of the tilde-case standard-cover vanishing.
3471	\end{lemma}
3472	\begin{proof}
3473	  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine, lem:qcoh_iso_tilde_sections}
3474	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_iso_tilde_sections} the quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) is isomorphic to
3475	  \(\widetilde{M}\) for \(M = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\), with sections over each face \(D(g_\sigma)\)
3476	  the away localisation \(M_{g_\sigma}\). The standard-cover {\v C}ech vanishing of
3477	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} applies to the tilde sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\): its section
3478	  {\v C}ech complex over any standard cover is exact in positive degrees. Transporting along the
3479	  isomorphism \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{M}\), which is natural in the coefficient and so induces
3480	  an isomorphism of section {\v C}ech complexes, gives \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\)
3481	  for all \(p > 0\) and every standard cover \(\mathcal{U}\). Hence \(\mathcal{F}\) has vanishing
3482	  higher {\v C}ech cohomology for the affine cover system.
3483	\end{proof}
3484	
3485	The {\v C}ech-to-cohomology comparison on a basis
3486	(Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}) is proved by a dimension shift on the
3487	coefficient module, decomposed below into four sub-lemmas: the short exact sequence of
3488	{\v C}ech complexes coming from a basis (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_ses_of_basis}), the
3489	resulting stability of vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology under the injective
3490	quotient (Lemma~\ref{lem:quotient_vanishing_cech}), the sheaf-cohomology base case
3491	\(H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_one_vanishing}), and the
3492	dimension-shift induction \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\)
3493	(Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_pos_vanishing}). The top lemma's proof then just
3494	chains these.

3496	\subsection{Section {\v C}ech infrastructure and the cover-system encoding}
3497	
3498	The sub-lemmas below are formalized in a \emph{cover-local}, presheaf-level, hypothesis-driven
3499	form: each lemma fixes a single index family \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) and works with the
3500	section {\v C}ech complexes of that one covering, taking the geometric inputs (section-level
3501	exactness on faces, positive-degree {\v C}ech vanishing) as hypotheses. The cover-global
3502	\((\mathcal{B}, \mathrm{Cov})\) data of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis} re-enters only
3503	in the inductive step (Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_pos_vanishing}), where it is packaged by
3504	the cover-system encoding (Definition~\ref{def:basis_cov_system},
3505	Definition~\ref{def:has_vanishing_higher_cech}) and discharges those hypotheses via the per-face
3506	short-exact-sequence derivation (Lemma~\ref{lem:face_ses_of_sheaf_ses}). The supporting
3507	definitions and the degreewise product engine are collected first.
3508	
3509	\begin{definition}
3510	
3511	\leanok
3512	[{\v C}ech cohomology accessor]
3513	  \label{def:cech_cohomology_accessor}
3514	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechCohomology}
3515	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex}
3516	  \textit{Project-local accessor.}
3517	  For an index family \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) (giving the covering \(\mathcal{U}\)), a
3518	  presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{F}\), and \(p \geq 0\), the \emph{{\v C}ech
3519	  cohomology} \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) is the degree-\(p\) homology of the section
3520	  {\v C}ech complex of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex},
3521	  \[
3522	    \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \;:=\;
3523	      H^p\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr),
3524	  \]
3525	  taken as an object of \(\mathrm{Ab}\). This is the named wrapper that the 01EO chain refers to
3526	  uniformly.
3527	\end{definition}
3528	
3529	\begin{definition}
3530	[Functoriality of the section {\v C}ech complex]
3531	  \label{def:section_cech_functoriality}
3532	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechCosimplicialMap,
3533	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechCosimplicialFunctor,
3534	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechComplexFunctor,
3535	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechComplexMap}
3536	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex}
3537	  \textit{Project-local.}
3538	  A morphism \(\varphi : \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{G}\) of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
3539	  acts on the section {\v C}ech data of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} coordinatewise:
3540	  on the basic-open section group of each intersection it is the map
3541	  \(\varphi(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}) : \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}) \to \mathcal{G}(U_{i_0 \ldots
3542	  i_p})\). These assemble into a cosimplicial morphism (the \emph{section {\v C}ech cosimplicial
3543	  map}), functorial in \(\varphi\) (the \emph{section {\v C}ech cosimplicial functor}). Composing
3544	  with the alternating-coface-map complex functor packages the section {\v C}ech complex as a
3545	  functor in the coefficient presheaf (the \emph{section {\v C}ech complex functor}) and yields,
3546	  for each \(\varphi\), the induced chain map of section {\v C}ech complexes
3547	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \varphi)\). These are the maps used to build the short
3548	  complex of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_short_complex}.
3549	\end{definition}
3550	
3551	\begin{definition}
3552	
3553	\leanok
3554	[Per-face section short complex]
3555	  \label{def:face_short_complex}
3556	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.faceShortComplex}
3557	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex}
3558	  \textit{Project-local building block.}
3559	  Let \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) be an index family and let
3560	  \(P : \mathcal{P}_1 \to \mathcal{P}_2 \to \mathcal{P}_3\) be a short complex of presheaves of
3561	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. For a degree \(p\) and a tuple \(\sigma : \{0, \ldots, p\} \to \iota\)
3562	  write \(V_\sigma = \bigcap_k U(\sigma k)\) for the corresponding basic open. The \emph{per-face
3563	  short complex} \(\mathrm{face}(U, P, \sigma)\) is the short complex of abelian groups obtained by
3564	  evaluating \(P\) on sections over \(V_\sigma\),
3565	  \[
3566	    \mathcal{P}_1(V_\sigma) \longrightarrow \mathcal{P}_2(V_\sigma) \longrightarrow
3567	      \mathcal{P}_3(V_\sigma),
3568	  \]
3569	  the maps being the section-level actions over \(V_\sigma\) of the two maps of \(P\). It is the
3570	  \((p, \sigma)\)-indexed factor whose product over all tuples \(\sigma\) is the degree-\(p\) term
3571	  of the section {\v C}ech complex.
3572	\end{definition}
3573	
3574	\begin{definition}
3575	
3576	\leanok
3577	[Short complex of section {\v C}ech complexes]
3578	  \label{def:section_cech_short_complex}
3579	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechComplexShortComplex}
3580	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:section_cech_functoriality}
3581	  \textit{Project-local.}
3582	  For an index family \(U\) and a short complex
3583	  \(P : \mathcal{P}_1 \to \mathcal{P}_2 \to \mathcal{P}_3\) of presheaves of
3584	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, the \emph{short complex of section {\v C}ech complexes} is
3585	  \[
3586	    0 \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{P}_1) \to
3587	      \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{P}_2) \to
3588	      \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{P}_3) \to 0,
3589	  \]
3590	  a short complex in the category of cochain complexes of abelian groups, with the two maps the
3591	  chain maps induced by the maps of \(P\) through the functoriality of
3592	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_functoriality}. Its short-exactness is the conclusion of
3593	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_ses_of_basis} and the input to Lemma~\ref{lem:quotient_vanishing_cech}.
3594	\end{definition}
3595	
3596	\begin{definition}
3597	
3598	\leanok
3599	[Cover system on a basis]
3600	  \label{def:basis_cov_system}
3601	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.BasisCovSystem, AlgebraicGeometry.CovDatum}
3602	  \uses{def:cech_complex, lem:ses_cech_h1, lem:injective_cech_acyclic}
3603	  \textit{Project-bespoke encoding of the hypotheses of Stacks 01EO.}
3604	  A \emph{cover system on a basis} for a scheme \(X\) is a record with five fields. A
3605	  \emph{covering datum} (the helper \(\mathrm{CovDatum}\)) is a pair \((\iota, U)\) of an index type
3606	  \(\iota\) together with an index family \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\); it is the raw shape
3607	  \(\Sigma\,\iota,\ \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) over which the section {\v C}ech complex is built.
3608	  The cover system bundles:
3609	  \begin{enumerate}
3610	  \item a basis \(\mathcal{B} \subseteq \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) for the topology;
3611	  \item a set \(\mathrm{Cov}\) of admissible coverings, each a \(\mathrm{CovDatum}\);
3612	  \item \emph{faces-in-basis} (\(\mathrm{faces\_mem}\)): for every covering in \(\mathrm{Cov}\) every
3613	    finite intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) of its opens lies in \(\mathcal{B}\) (condition (1) of
3614	    Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis});
3615	  \item \emph{section surjectivity} (\(\mathrm{surj\_of\_vanishing}\)): for every \(V \in \mathcal{B}\)
3616	    and every short exact sequence \(S : 0 \to S_1 \to S_2 \to S_3 \to 0\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
3617	    whose left term \(S_1\) has vanishing positive {\v C}ech cohomology over the coverings of \(V\) in
3618	    \(\mathrm{Cov}\), the section map \(S_2(V) \to S_3(V)\) is surjective;
3619	  \item \emph{injective acyclicity} (\(\mathrm{injective\_acyclic}\)): for every injective
3620	    \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{I}\) and every covering \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathrm{Cov}\) the
3621	    positive-degree {\v C}ech cohomology vanishes, \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for
3622	    all \(p > 0\).
3623	  \end{enumerate}
3624	  Unlike a bare combinatorial record, the structure carries two sheaf-theoretic ({\v C}ech-cohomology)
3625	  fields. The field \(\mathrm{surj\_of\_vanishing}\) is \emph{not} the raw cofinality datum: it is the
3626	  section-surjectivity \emph{output} that cofinality together with Lemma~\ref{lem:ses_cech_h1}
3627	  produces, packaged directly as the consequence the 01EO chain consumes. The field
3628	  \(\mathrm{injective\_acyclic}\) is the injective {\v C}ech-acyclicity of
3629	  Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} for the system's own coverings. These are exactly the data
3630	  that the dimension-shift induction Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_pos_vanishing} consumes.
3631	\end{definition}
3632	
3633	\begin{definition}
3634	
3635	\leanok
3636	[Vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for a cover system]
3637	  \label{def:has_vanishing_higher_cech}
3638	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.HasVanishingHigherCech}
3639	  \uses{def:basis_cov_system, def:cech_cohomology_accessor}
3640	  \textit{Project-bespoke inductive predicate (condition (3) of Stacks 01EO, per module).}
3641	  Given a cover system \(s\) on a basis (Definition~\ref{def:basis_cov_system}), an
3642	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{has vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for
3643	  \(s\)} when
3644	  \[
3645	    \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0 \qquad
3646	      \text{for every } \mathcal{U} \in s.\mathrm{Cov} \text{ and every } p > 0,
3647	  \]
3648	  with \(\check{H}^p\) the accessor of Definition~\ref{def:cech_cohomology_accessor}. This is the
3649	  per-module form of condition (3). It is deliberately stated for an \emph{arbitrary}
3650	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module: the dimension-shift induction of
3651	  Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_pos_vanishing} feeds the quotient \(\mathcal{Q} =
3652	  \mathcal{I}/\mathcal{F}\) back as the coefficient, and \(\mathcal{Q}\) need not be quasi-coherent
3653	  even when \(\mathcal{F}\) is. The inductive class is therefore ``\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules with
3654	  vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for \(s\)'', a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) entering only
3655	  as the seed at the affine (Tag 02KG) instantiation; the predicate must \emph{not} be specialized
3656	  to quasi-coherent modules.
3657	\end{definition}
3658	
3659	\begin{lemma}
3660	[Product of short exact sequences in \(\mathrm{Ab}\) (AB4*)]
3661	  \label{lem:short_exact_pi_map}
3662	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.shortExact_piMap, AlgebraicGeometry.pi_π_map_apply}
3663	  \uses{def:cech_complex}
3664	  \textit{Project AB4* lemma (Archon-original infrastructure).}
3665	  Let \((S_j)_{j \in J}\) be a family, indexed by an arbitrary type \(J\), of short exact sequences
3666	  of abelian groups, \(S_j : 0 \to A_j \xrightarrow{f_j} B_j \xrightarrow{g_j} C_j \to 0\). Then
3667	  the short complex assembled from the product maps,
3668	  \[
3669	    0 \to \prod_{j \in J} A_j \xrightarrow{\;\prod_j f_j\;} \prod_{j \in J} B_j
3670	      \xrightarrow{\;\prod_j g_j\;} \prod_{j \in J} C_j \to 0,
3671	  \]
3672	  is again short exact. This is the AB4* exactness-of-products property of \(\mathrm{Ab}\); it is
3673	  the degreewise engine of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_ses_of_basis}, since each {\v C}ech term is a product
3674	  of basic-open section groups (Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex}).
3675	\end{lemma}
3676	\begin{proof}
3677	  Monomorphy of the product map \(\prod_j f_j\) is automatic, as products preserve monomorphisms.
3678	  Exactness at the middle is checked componentwise: an element of \(\prod_j B_j\) annihilated by
3679	  \(\prod_j g_j\) is annihilated in each coordinate \(j\), hence lies in the image of \(f_j\) by
3680	  exactness of \(S_j\), and the resulting coordinatewise preimages assemble to a preimage in
3681	  \(\prod_j A_j\). The one nontrivial point is epimorphy of \(\prod_j g_j\), which is \emph{not} an
3682	  automatic instance in \(\mathrm{Ab}\): given a target tuple in \(\prod_j C_j\), surjectivity of
3683	  each \(g_j\) supplies a coordinatewise preimage, and these assemble — through the canonical
3684	  identification of an \(\mathrm{Ab}\)-valued product with the set-theoretic product of its
3685	  components — to a preimage of the whole tuple. Hence \(\prod_j g_j\) is an epimorphism and the
3686	  displayed sequence is short exact.
3687	\end{proof}

3752	  verify short-exactness in each cohomological degree \(p\). In degree \(p\) the term of the
3753	  section {\v C}ech complex is the product \(\prod_{\sigma}(-)(V_\sigma)\) over all tuples
3754	  \(\sigma : \{0, \ldots, p\} \to \iota\) of section groups over the basic opens \(V_\sigma\), and
3755	  the two maps are the products of the corresponding per-face maps; thus the degree-\(p\) short
3756	  complex is the product over \(\sigma\) of the per-face short complexes
3757	  (Definition~\ref{def:face_short_complex}). Each of these is short exact by hypothesis, so their
3758	  product is short exact by the AB4* product-of-short-exact-sequences lemma
3759	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:short_exact_pi_map}). As this holds in every degree, the short complex of section
3760	  {\v C}ech complexes (Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_short_complex}) is short exact.
3761	\end{proof}

3782	  Let \(S : 0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of

3786	  supplied by Lemma~\ref{lem:ses_cech_h1}. Writing \(P\) for the section short complex of \(S\)

3789	  \(\sigma\). This lemma produces the per-face hypothesis consumed by

3796	  \leanok

3798	  \(\mathcal{F}(V) \to \mathcal{I}(V)\) and exactness at the middle of \(\mathcal{F}(V) \to
3799	  \mathcal{I}(V) \to \mathcal{Q}(V)\) are the left-exactness of the sections functor applied to the
3800	  short exact sequence of sheaves \(S\): the sections functor \(\mathcal{G} \mapsto \mathcal{G}(V)\)
3801	  is the composite of the inclusion of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules into presheaves of
3802	  modules (a right adjoint, hence preserving finite limits) with evaluation at \(V\) (which
3803	  preserves limits), so it is left exact and carries the exactness of \(S\) to exactness of the
3804	  section sequence at \(\mathcal{I}(V)\). The remaining map \(\mathcal{I}(V) \to \mathcal{Q}(V)\) is
3805	  surjective by hypothesis, i.e. by the output of Lemma~\ref{lem:ses_cech_h1} at the basic open
3806	  \(V\). Hence the per-face short complex is short exact.
