3816	  \textit{Abstract homological core of Lemma~\ref{lem:quotient_vanishing_cech}.}
3817	  Let \(T : 0 \to T_1 \to T_2 \to T_3 \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of cochain complexes
3818	  (cohomological \(\mathbb{N}\)-grading) of abelian groups, and suppose the middle term \(T_2\) and
3819	  the left term \(T_1\) both have vanishing positive homology: \(H^p(T_2) = 0\) and \(H^p(T_1) = 0\)
3820	  for all \(p > 0\). Then the right term \(T_3\) also has vanishing positive homology, \(H^p(T_3) =
3821	  0\) for all \(p > 0\). This is the section-Čech-free homological core consumed by
3822	  Lemma~\ref{lem:quotient_vanishing_cech}.

3824	\begin{proof}
3825	  \uses{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
3826	  \leanok
3827	  Fix \(p > 0\). In the homology long exact sequence of \(T\)
3828	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) the two homology groups of the middle term
3829	  adjacent to the connecting map at \(T_3\) in degree \(p\), namely \(H^p(T_2)\) and \(H^{p+1}(T_2)\),
3830	  both vanish (positive degrees, by the hypothesis on \(T_2\)); hence the connecting homomorphism is
3831	  an isomorphism \(H^p(T_3) \cong H^{p+1}(T_1)\). The right-hand side vanishes since \(p + 1 > 0\)
3832	  and \(T_1\) has vanishing positive homology. Therefore \(H^p(T_3) = 0\).
3833	\end{proof}

3990	  Induct on \(p \ge 1\) with the statement quantified over \emph{all} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
3991	  \(\mathcal{F}\) having vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for \(s\)
3992	  (Definition~\ref{def:has_vanishing_higher_cech}) simultaneously, so that the quotient may take the

3995	  \(\mathcal{Q} = \mathcal{I}/\mathcal{F}\), giving a short exact sequence \(S : 0 \to \mathcal{F}
3996	  \to \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0\).

3999	  every \(\mathcal{U} \in s.\mathrm{Cov}\), the faces-in-basis field \(\mathrm{faces\_mem}\) (condition
4000	  (1)) places every face \(V_\sigma\) in \(s.\mathcal{B}\); the section-surjectivity field
4001	  \(\mathrm{surj\_of\_vanishing}\) of the cover system, applied to the short exact sequence \(S\) at

4005	  (Definition~\ref{def:has_vanishing_higher_cech}); mathematically this field packages
4006	  Lemma~\ref{lem:ses_cech_h1} (cofinality plus the {\v C}ech \(\check{H}^1\)-surjectivity criterion).

4009	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_ses_of_basis} gives the short exact sequence of section {\v C}ech complexes for
4010	  \(\mathcal{U}\); in particular the section surjectivity \(\mathcal{I}(U) \to \mathcal{Q}(U)\) holds
4011	  at every basic open \(U \in s.\mathcal{B}\) (take \(\mathcal{U}\) a covering of \(U\) in
4012	  \(s.\mathrm{Cov}\) and the length-one face). Feeding the {\v C}ech short exact sequence into
4013	  Lemma~\ref{lem:quotient_vanishing_cech} — with \(\check{H}^{>0}(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\)
4014	  from injective acyclicity and \(\check{H}^{>0}(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) from
4015	  \(\mathcal{F}\)'s hypothesis — shows \(\check{H}^{>0}(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{Q}) = 0\) for every
4016	  \(\mathcal{U} \in s.\mathrm{Cov}\); that is, \(\mathcal{Q}\) again has vanishing higher {\v C}ech
4017	  cohomology for \(s\), so it is itself a valid instance of the inductive statement.
4018	
4019	  \emph{Base case \(p = 1\):} apply Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_one_vanishing} to \(S\) at
4020	  the basic open \(U \in s.\mathcal{B}\), its section-surjectivity hypothesis discharged by the
4021	  paragraph above.
4022	
4023	  \emph{Step \(p \Rightarrow p+1\):} the covariant Ext long exact sequence
4024	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_covariant_les_mathlib}) of
4025	  \(0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0\) at \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\)
4026	  gives the exact strand
4027	  \[
4028	    H^p(U, \mathcal{Q}) \xrightarrow{\ \delta\ } H^{p+1}(U, \mathcal{F}) \to
4029	      H^{p+1}(U, \mathcal{I}).
4030	  \]
4031	  The right term vanishes since \(\mathcal{I}\) is injective
4032	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib}), and the left term
4033	  \(H^p(U, \mathcal{Q}) = 0\) by the inductive hypothesis applied to \(\mathcal{Q}\)
4034	  (admissible by the previous paragraph). Exactness forces
4035	  \(H^{p+1}(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\). This completes the induction, giving
4036	  \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\) and all \(U \in s.\mathcal{B}\).
4037	\end{proof}

4187	\section{The {\v C}ech complex computes $R^i f_*$}
4188	
4189	The comparison is proved by the \emph{acyclic-resolution} (Cartan--Leray) route. The
4190	augmented {\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) whose terms are
4191	acyclic for \(f_*\); the abstract homological-algebra
4192	Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} then identifies the derived
4193	functor with the cohomology of the complex obtained by applying \(f_*\). The two
4194	geometric inputs are isolated in the following sub-lemmas.
4195	
4196	\begin{lemma}
4197	[The augmented {\v C}ech complex is a resolution]
4198	  \label{lem:cech_augmented_resolution}
4199	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechAugmented_exact}
4200	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, lem:cech_acyclic_affine}
4201	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
4202	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof: exactness of the
4203	  % extended {\v C}ech complex) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L68--82).
4204	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We are asked to show that the extended complex
4205	  % \begin{equation}
4206	  % \label{equation-extended}
4207	  % 0 \to
4208	  % M \to
4209	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
4210	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
4211	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
4212	  % \ldots
4213	  % \end{equation}
4214	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
4215	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact.
4216	  % It suffices to show that (\ref{equation-extended})
4217	  % is exact after localizing at a prime $\mathfrak p$, see
4218	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-characterize-zero-local}."
4219	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
4220	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof).}
4221	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
4222	  \(\mathfrak{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with all
4223	  intersections affine. Form the augmented {\v C}ech complex on \(X\),
4224	  \[
4225	    0 \;\to\; \mathcal{F} \;\to\; \mathcal{C}^0 \;\to\; \mathcal{C}^1 \;\to\;
4226	    \mathcal{C}^2 \;\to\; \cdots,
4227	    \qquad
4228	    \mathcal{C}^p \;=\; \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
4229	      (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*\bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
4230	  \]
4231	  the degree-\(p\) term being the degree-\(p\) object of the {\v C}ech nerve of
4232	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}, with augmentation the canonical map
4233	  \(\mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{C}^0\). This augmented complex is exact; that is, the
4234	  {\v C}ech nerve is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\).
4235	\end{lemma}
4236	
4237	\begin{proof}
4238	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, lem:cech_acyclic_affine}
4239	  Exactness of a complex of sheaves is a local question on \(X\), so it may be
4240	  checked on the stalks, equivalently over each affine open of a basis. Restricting
4241	  to an affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) on which \(\mathcal{F}|_U =
4242	  \widetilde{M}\) and over which the cover restricts to a standard cover \(U =
4243	  \bigcup D(f_i)\), the augmented {\v C}ech complex becomes the extended complex of
4244	  localisations
4245	  \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to
4246	  \cdots\). Its exactness is exactly the standard-cover vanishing of
4247	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}, witnessed by the prime-local contracting
4248	  homotopy \(h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\mathrm{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}\). As this
4249	  holds over every affine of the basis, the augmented complex is exact on \(X\).
4250	\end{proof}

4252	\begin{lemma}
4253	[Cohomology sheaf is the sheafification of the objectwise homology]
4254	  \label{def:cohomology_sheaf_is_sheafify_homology}
4255	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.homologyIsoSheafify,
4256	    PresheafOfModules.counitComplexIso,
4257	    PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdditive,
4258	    CategoryTheory.Functor.mapHomologyIso'}
4259	  % NOTE: project-built generalization. Stacks Tag~01XJ
4260	  % (\texttt{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}) establishes the consequence
4261	  % $R^if_*\mathcal{F} \cong \text{sheafification of }(V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V),\mathcal{F}))$
4262	  % but does not state sheafification commuting with the homology of an arbitrary
4263	  % cochain complex as a standalone lemma. No verbatim Stacks source quote for this block.
4264	  Let \(K\) be a cochain complex of presheaves of modules over a site admitting
4265	  sheafification. For every index \(i\) there is a natural
4266	  isomorphism between the \(i\)-th homology of the sheafified complex and the
4267	  sheafification of the \(i\)-th objectwise (presheaf-level) homology of \(K\),
4268	  \[
4269	    H^i\bigl(\operatorname{sheafify}(K)\bigr)
4270	      \;\cong\;
4271	    \operatorname{sheafify}\bigl(H^i(K)\bigr).
4272	  \]
4273	  Equivalently, sheafification commutes with the formation of homology. This is the
4274	  reusable engine underlying the presheaf description of the higher direct images
4275	  (Stacks Tag~01XJ): it is what turns the resolution-internal cohomology sheaf into a
4276	  sheafification of an explicit cohomology presheaf.
4277	\end{lemma}
4278	
4279	\begin{proof}
4280	  Sheafification is exact: as the left adjoint of the inclusion of sheaves into
4281	  presheaves it preserves all colimits, and on a site admitting sheafification it
4282	  additionally preserves finite limits, so it preserves both the kernels and the
4283	  cokernels through which homology is computed. An exact additive functor commutes with
4284	  the homology functor of a cochain complex; chasing this through the counit complex
4285	  isomorphism \(\operatorname{sheafify}(\iota K) \cong K\) (the inclusion–sheafification
4286	  adjunction counit, an isomorphism on sheaves) gives
4287	  \(H^i(K) \cong H^i(\operatorname{sheafify} K) \cong
4288	  \operatorname{sheafify}(H^i(K))\). The supporting bricks are the
4289	  additivity of sheafification, the counit complex isomorphism, and the
4290	  complex-level transport of the short-complex map–homology isomorphism along an
4291	  additive homology-preserving functor.
4292	\end{proof}

4294	\begin{lemma}
4295	[Presheaf description of the higher direct images]
4296	  \label{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}
4297	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage_iso_sheafify_presheafHomology,
4298	    AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardResolutionPresheafComplex}
4299	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image, def:cohomology_sheaf_is_sheafify_homology}
4300	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Tag 01XJ, Lemma
4301	  % \texttt{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images} (the higher direct images are the
4302	  % sheafifications of the cohomology presheaves of preimages) (read from
4303	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L591--603).
4304	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to Y$ be a morphism of ringed spaces.
4305	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
4306	  % The sheaves $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ are the sheaves
4307	  % associated to the presheaves
4308	  % $$
4309	  % V \longmapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F})
4310	  % $$
4311	  % with restriction mappings as in Equation (\ref{equation-restriction-mapping}).
4312	  % There is a similar statement for $R^if_*$ applied to a
4313	  % bounded below complex $\mathcal{F}^\bullet$."
4314	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, Tag 01XJ
4315	  (\texttt{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}).}
4316	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{G}\) an
4317	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and assume the category of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules has
4318	  enough injective objects, so that the derived pushforward \(R^k f_*\) is defined.
4319	  Choose an injective resolution \(\mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet\) in
4320	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and push it forward degreewise to obtain the cochain complex
4321	  of presheaves of modules \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) on \(S\). For every \(k \geq 0\)
4322	  the higher direct image \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G}\) is isomorphic to the
4323	  \emph{sheafification of the presheaf of objectwise homology} of this pushed complex,
4324	  \[
4325	    R^k f_*\mathcal{G}
4326	      \;\cong\;
4327	    \operatorname{sheafify}\Bigl(\,V \longmapsto
4328	      H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr)\,\Bigr),
4329	    \qquad V \subseteq S \text{ open},
4330	  \]
4331	  the sheafification of the \(k\)-th presheaf-level homology of
4332	  \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\). As a closing remark, this gives the affine-local
4333	  vanishing criterion consumed downstream
4334	  (Lemmas~\ref{lem:open_immersion_pushforward_comp} and
4335	  \ref{lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic}): since the sheafification of a presheaf
4336	  vanishes exactly when that presheaf vanishes on a basis of opens,
4337	  \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G} = 0\) iff \(H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr) = 0\)
4338	  for every \(V\) in a basis of the topology of \(S\); the affine opens form such a
4339	  basis, so it suffices to check the vanishing on affine \(V\).
4340	
4341	  The identification of the displayed presheaf homology with the absolute cohomology
4342	  of the preimage,
4343	  \[
4344	    H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr)
4345	      \;=\;
4346	    H^k\bigl(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
4347	  \]
4348	  which uses that \(\mathcal{I}^\bullet|_{f^{-1}(V)}\) is again injective over
4349	  \(f^{-1}(V)\), is supplied at point of use; this lemma records the
4350	  resolution-internal sheafify-of-objectwise-homology form.
4351	\end{lemma}
4352	
4353	\begin{proof}
4354	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image, def:cohomology_sheaf_is_sheafify_homology}
4355	  Choose an injective resolution \(\mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet\) in
4356	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. By definition \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G}\) is the \(k\)-th
4357	  cohomology sheaf of the complex \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) of presheaves of modules
4358	  over \(S\). The comparison engine of
4359	  Lemma~\ref{def:cohomology_sheaf_is_sheafify_homology} now applies: taking homology in
4360	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules is the sheafification of the objectwise (presheaf-level)
4361	  homology, because the sheafification/inclusion adjunction is exact (it preserves the
4362	  finite limits and colimits computing kernels and cokernels). Hence
4363	  \[
4364	    R^k f_*\mathcal{G}
4365	      \;\cong\;
4366	    \operatorname{sheafify}\Bigl(\,V \longmapsto
4367	      H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr)\,\Bigr),
4368	  \]
4369	  which is the stated isomorphism. Since the sheafification of a presheaf vanishes
4370	  exactly when the underlying presheaf vanishes on a basis of opens, this yields the
4371	  affine-local vanishing criterion.

512	\medskip\noindent
513	Recall that $\Spec(R)$ has a basis of open sets $D(f)$,

516	In addition, the intersection of two standard opens is another:

519	\begin{lemma}
520	\label{lemma-standard-open}
521	Let $R$ be a ring. Let $f \in R$.
522	\begin{enumerate}
523	\item If $g\in R$ and $D(g) \subset D(f)$, then
524	\begin{enumerate}
525	\item $f$ is invertible in $R_g$,
526	\item $g^e = af$ for some $e \geq 1$ and $a \in R$,
527	\item there is a canonical ring map $R_f \to R_g$, and
528	\item there is a canonical $R_f$-module map
529	$M_f \to M_g$ for any $R$-module $M$.
530	\end{enumerate}
531	\item Any open covering of $D(f)$ can be refined to a finite
532	open covering of the form $D(f) = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(g_i)$.
533	\item If $g_1, \ldots, g_n \in R$, then $D(f) \subset \bigcup D(g_i)$
534	if and only if $g_1, \ldots, g_n$ generate the unit ideal in $R_f$.
535	\end{enumerate}
536	\end{lemma}
537	
538	\begin{proof}
539	Recall that $D(g) = \Spec(R_g)$ (see
540	Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-standard-open}).
541	Thus (a) holds because $f$
542	maps to an element of $R_g$ which is not
543	contained in any prime ideal, and hence invertible,
544	see Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-Zariski-topology}.
545	Write the inverse of $f$ in $R_g$ as $a/g^d$.
546	This means $g^d - af$ is annihilated by a power of $g$, whence (b).
547	For (c), the map $R_f \to R_g$ exists by (a) from the universal property
548	of localization, or we can define it by mapping $b/f^n$
549	to $a^nb/g^{ne}$. The equality $M_f = M \otimes_R R_f$
550	can be used to obtain the map on modules, or
551	we can define $M_f \to M_g$ by mapping
552	$x/f^n$ to $a^nx/g^{ne}$.
553	
554	\medskip\noindent
555	Recall that $D(f)$ is quasi-compact, see
556	Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-qc-open}.
557	Hence the second statement follows directly
558	from the fact that the standard opens form
559	a basis for the topology.

561	\medskip\noindent
